result: Sometimes I imagine the city as a patchwork of secret breathing rooms hidden behind brick facades. They are tiny, often improvised, yet they rearrange the map inside my mind. A courtyard draped in wisteria can hush the sirens even before the petals move in the wind. Another day it might be an elderly neighbor who coaxes tomatoes from paint buckets placed along the roofline. I pause to trade compliments with her, and in that exchange I feel the city soften at its seams. Maybe the reason I am drawn to these spaces is that they let me practice the art of temporary belonging. There is always the knowledge that the lease will expire, the landlord will sell, the scaffolding will return. Yet in the meantime, moss learns the texture of glass, and young children learn the names of herbs they crush between their fingers. I take photographs almost obsessively, not for social media, but to remember how the light bruised the brick at noon. Later, when insomnia turns the ceiling into a projection screen, those images become a whispered travelogue. They remind me that tenderness can be engineered, that even concrete can learn to host a habitat. Tomorrow I will probably discover another doorway ajar, another improbable fern, another invitation to linger before the world resumes its hurry. When that happens, I plan to carry a pocketful of seeds, nothing special, just marigolds from my mother, so that each pause leaves a trace brighter than the fading graffiti behind walls.
